answer:
**Viṣṇu Smṛti Chapter 2 does not contain a section that lists the duties of a king toward his subjects.**

This chapter instead outlines the duties (*dharma*) and means of livelihood for the four *varṇas* (social orders): Brāhmaṇa, Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, and Śūdra.[^1]

The relevant portions for the Kṣatriya (the warrior class, which includes kings) are as follows:

> [p-f4e97b] The duty of a Kṣatriya is constant practice with arms. // Vi_2.6 //

> [p-e4d407] For a Kṣatriya, the protection of the earth. // Vi_2.12 //

*"Protection of the earth"* implies safeguarding subjects, but there is no explicit list of a king's duties toward them here.[^2]

**The duties of a king (*rājadharma*)—including toward subjects—are addressed in Chapter 3,** which opens: "Now, the duties of a king (*rājadharma*). // Vi_3.1 // The protection of..."[^3]

[^1]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, Chapter 2 (full), [translation](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/02/translation/).

[^2]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, Chapter 2, [paragraphs p-f4e97b to p-e4d407](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/02/translation/#p-f4e97b).

[^3]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, Chapter 3, [opening](https://falsafa-corpus.org/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/03/translation/#p-641d99).

*(Note: Exact citation URLs are derived from tool outputs; chapter-level from `read_chapter`, paragraph-specific from embedded IDs requiring `get_passage` for full highlighting. No `get_passage` called here as no direct quote from ch. 2 matches the query.)*
